host-2016-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The Colic passage reports a study: 73 percent of children with migraine had been colicky babies, versus 27 percent of controls, and 'the numbers suggest that migraine and colic may indeed be related'. The headline is the colic-to-migraine connection — answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the 'main point' of the Colic paragraph. Main-point questions want the one idea that ties the whole text together, not a vivid detail. Read the whole paragraph once and then ask: if I had to write the headline in one sentence, what would it be?

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the argument. The setup is 'Anna Zanin... noticed that babies with colic and children with migraine share certain symptoms, she decided to explore a possible association.' The closing nails the conclusion: 'the numbers suggest that migraine and colic may indeed be related.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: a doctor noticed colicky babies and migraine kids had similar symptoms, ran a study, found a big statistical gap (73% vs 27%), and concluded the two conditions are probably linked. The trajectory is observation → study → tentative link.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Colic' = inconsolable crying in an otherwise healthy infant — defined in the first sentence. 'Migraine' = a severe, recurring headache. 'Causal link' = one thing actually causes the other (stronger than mere correlation). 'Controls' = the comparison group in a study, here children without migraine.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option against the paraphrase. A says a path to solving migraine has been found — but the passage is about a possible CAUSE link with colic, not about treating migraine. B says Zanin discovered colicky children 'actually suffer from migraine' — too strong; Zanin emphasises 'further evidence is needed' for a causal link. C says parents of colicky babies often have migraine — the passage tracks the CHILDREN, not the parents. D says colic in infancy seems connected to migraine later — that matches the closing line exactly.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The Zanin study is about the colic-to-migraine connection across the same child's life, framed cautiously as 'may indeed be related' — which is exactly what D paraphrases.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'mystery' and 'study' together and assume a breakthrough in understanding migraine itself.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage is about correlation between two conditions — not about cracking the mystery of what causes migraine in the first place. Step 3's paraphrase has no claim about understanding migraine's mechanism.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the 73 percent figure and read it as 'colicky kids ARE migraine sufferers'.
distractors.why_wrong: Zanin explicitly says 'further evidence is needed to determine whether there is a causal link' — she found correlation, not identity. Step 5 shows the hedged 'may indeed be related' phrasing rules B out.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'migraine in the family' is to think of inheritance from parents.
distractors.why_wrong: The study tracked the children's OWN history (were they colicky as babies?), not their parents' migraine status. Step 2's locate step shows the passage never mentions parents.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-031.technique: On main-point questions in ELF, find the closing sentence that names the link or shift — it almost always paraphrases the correct option. Watch for hedge words like 'suggest', 'may', 'seems' — they're a signal the headline is a TENTATIVE LINK, not a strong claim.
host-2016-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: Strong numbers (73 percent vs 27 percent) tempt strong claims (B's 'actually suffer'). The author's own hedged phrasing ('further evidence is needed', 'may indeed be related') tells you the right option must be hedged too.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The Lamarck passage shows biologists today still openly hostile to his name ('Crazy Lamarckian shit', 'the dirty word here... is... Jean-Baptiste Lamarck'). The closing line — 'may yet give Lamarck's name a sheen of respectability' — is only a hedged possibility. The dominant present-tense view is still skepticism — answer A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what 'fits best with this description of the ideas of Jean-Baptiste Lamarck'. The phrasing 'fits best' means: which option captures the OVERALL stance the passage takes toward Lamarck? Read for the arc of his reputation, not a single sentence.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Three turns in the passage. Opening: 'Crazy Lamarckian shit' shows hostility from biologists today. Middle: his mechanism 'was discredited' because there's no genetic basis. Closing: new mouse research 'may yet give Lamarck's name a sheen of respectability'. Reputation: down, then potentially up again.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Lamarck argued in the 1700s that traits acquired in life can be passed to children. Biologists rejected this because no genetic mechanism existed. Now epigenetics suggests something Lamarck-like might actually happen in mice. So his standing has gone: respected → mocked → maybe redeemed. Not a steady line — opinions have moved back and forth.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Acquired traits' = characteristics gained during life (not inherited at birth), like a callus from work. 'Discredited' = officially rejected, dismissed as wrong. 'Epigenetics' = changes that turn genes on or off without altering the genes themselves. 'A sheen of respectability' = a fresh appearance of being taken seriously.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says his ideas are STILL regarded with skepticism in biological circles — matches the present-tense hostility 'Crazy Lamarckian shit' and 'to many biologists, the dirty word is... Jean-Baptiste Lamarck'; the closing sentence only hedges that this MIGHT change. B says opinions have shifted BACK AND FORTH since the 1700s — overstates a single swing into a repeated oscillation; the passage shows one rejection followed by a tentative possible re-appraisal. C says his ideas are CLEARLY INCOMPATIBLE with modern evolution — too strong; the passage says new findings 'fit with natural selection'. D says recent studies show his explanation was entirely correct — too strong the other way; the hedged 'may yet' rules this out.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. Biologists today still openly mock the Lamarckian label ('Crazy Lamarckian shit', 'the dirty word'), and the closing 'may yet give... a sheen of respectability' is only a hedged possibility — so the dominant view remains skepticism.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the back-and-forth arc — discredited, then possibly redeemed — as a pattern of repeated reversal.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage shows only ONE swing (rejected, with a tentative recent twist), not a centuries-long oscillation. Step 3 shows the current state is still skepticism with a hint of possible change.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'his mechanism can't be the basis for natural selection' and read it as a total incompatibility verdict.
distractors.why_wrong: That sentence is followed by 'Now new work suggests... it fits with natural selection.' Step 2 shows the passage does NOT close with incompatibility — it closes with a possible reconciliation.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'mice may pass on memories' is to read it as proof Lamarck was right.
distractors.why_wrong: The closing sentence is hedged twice: 'may pass on' and 'may yet give... a sheen of respectability'. Step 4's vocabulary check on 'a sheen of respectability' shows that's a possibility, not a confirmed correctness.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-032.technique: On 'attitude' or 'fits best' items, look for the overall TONE the author leaves on the topic, not the strongest single sentence. Hedge words ('may', 'might', 'a sheen of') tell you the right option will be hedged too — and that the dominant current view is usually the one most often repeated.
host-2016-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: Closing sentences with 'may yet' or 'a sheen of' look like turnarounds but only signal a tentative possibility. The bulk of the passage's evidence still defines the dominant view — here, the biologists' open hostility.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The Moral Climates passage says of 18th-century England: 'a surprisingly transgressive place. Pornographic literature was popular... erotic manuals were read aloud in alehouses... Homosexuality was fashionable among gentlemen. Contraceptives were sold brazenly in Covent Garden.' That's a portrait of liberal attitudes that look much like today's — answer A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what the passage tells us about 'moral attitudes in 18th-century England'. Find the paragraph that describes Georgian England specifically (before the Victorian shift) and gather the concrete details. The right option will be the one that BEST FITS the cluster of evidence — not the one that captures a single sentence.

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: The relevant paragraph opens 'England in the 18th century was a surprisingly transgressive place.' Then: 'Pornographic literature was popular and erotic manuals were read aloud in alehouses. Homosexuality was fashionable among gentlemen. Contraceptives were sold brazenly in Covent Garden.' Four concrete sexual-freedom markers in one paragraph.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Georgian England was sexually open in public ways that look strikingly modern: erotic books circulated freely, gay relationships were fashionable in elite circles, contraception was openly sold. The author's whole point is that this looks much closer to today than the Victorian era that came after.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Transgressive' = breaking conventional moral rules. 'Brazenly' = openly, without shame. 'Fashionable' (in this context) = socially accepted in the elite class. 'Gentlemen' = upper-class men in 18th-century usage. The collective tone of these words is open, public, unembarrassed sexuality.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says they were 'in many ways as liberal as those of today' — matches the framing 'surprisingly transgressive' and the four modern-looking examples. B says sex was 'seldom brought up in public' — the OPPOSITE of what the passage shows (erotic manuals read aloud in alehouses, contraceptives sold openly). C says there were 'great differences between the upper and lower classes' — the passage groups gentlemen, alehouse listeners, and Covent Garden shoppers together rather than contrasting them. D says gay relationships were 'outlawed but frequent' — the passage says they were 'fashionable among gentlemen', not outlawed.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The whole point of the Georgian paragraph is that 18th-century England looked, in its public sexual mores, surprisingly like today — that's the contrast the author needs before describing the Victorian crackdown.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to imagine all pre-modern eras as sexually repressed and assume 18th-century England was no exception.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly inverts that assumption with concrete public examples — erotic manuals read aloud, contraceptives sold openly. Step 2's evidence is the opposite of 'seldom brought up in public'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'gentlemen' and 'alehouses' and assume the passage is contrasting upper and lower classes.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage gives upper-class and popular examples side by side as part of one continuous picture of openness, not as a class contrast. Step 5 shows the author groups them, not separates them.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on historical homosexuality is to assume it was always illegal even when practised.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says it was 'fashionable among gentlemen' — a positive social marker in elite circles, not an outlawed practice. Step 4's vocabulary check on 'fashionable' rules this out.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-033.technique: On 'what are we told about X' items, gather the CLUSTER of concrete details about X in the passage, then pick the option that summarises the cluster. One vivid detail can mislead — match against the whole set.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The Moral Climates passage says of the Victorians: 'Sex did not vanish in Victorian England but it was certainly idealised and privatised in a novel manner. The Victorians scrubbed the public sphere clean of sensuality.' Their OFFICIAL public attitude was therefore more restrictive than the Georgians' — answer B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what the passage says 'about the Victorians in relation to sex'. The passage contrasts Victorians with Georgians (the previous question's era), so the right option will mark a SHIFT from Georgian openness — without overstating it.

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: Two sentences carry the description: 'Of course the Victorians were every bit as prone to vice as the Georgians had been — even Queen Victoria collected nude male figure drawings. Sex did not vanish in Victorian England but it was certainly idealised and privatised in a novel manner. The Victorians scrubbed the public sphere clean of sensuality.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Victorians privately still did the same things Georgians did — Queen Victoria herself collected erotic art. What changed was the PUBLIC side: sex was hidden, idealised, kept indoors, and the public sphere was deliberately cleaned of any sensuality. So the OFFICIAL position was strict while private life stayed messy.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Idealised' = held up as a pure or perfect version, removed from messy reality. 'Privatised' (here) = pushed into the private sphere, hidden from public view. 'Scrubbed clean of sensuality' = removed all openly sexual material from public life. 'Prone to vice' = inclined toward immoral behaviour. The key contrast is between PUBLIC face (strict) and PRIVATE behaviour (still messy).

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says they 'banished it from public AS WELL AS private life' — too strong; the passage explicitly says 'sex did not vanish' and Queen Victoria collected erotica. B says their 'official attitude was clearly more restrictive than that of the Georgians' — matches 'scrubbed the public sphere clean' as a contrast with Georgian openness. C says they viewed sex as 'a regrettable aspect of married life' — the passage talks about social regulation, not the Victorians' personal attitudes within marriage. D says public morality 'hardly differed from that of the previous period' — the OPPOSITE of the central contrast the paragraph builds.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The passage's key move is to split private behaviour (still messy) from official, public attitude (now strict). Only B captures that public restrictiveness as the distinctive Victorian shift.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'scrubbed the public sphere clean' and 'idealised' as a total ban on sex.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage opens that very paragraph with 'Sex did not vanish in Victorian England' and 'every bit as prone to vice as the Georgians had been'. Step 3 separates public face from private behaviour — A collapses them.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stock images of the Victorians frame sex as duty rather than pleasure within marriage.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't describe Victorians' internal attitudes toward marital sex — it describes how sex was handled IN PUBLIC. Step 5 shows the paragraph is about social regulation, not married couples' private feelings.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the line 'every bit as prone to vice as the Georgians', D feels like a fair summary — same behaviour, same morality.
distractors.why_wrong: That line refers to PRIVATE behaviour. The very next sentences describe a dramatic PUBLIC shift ('idealised and privatised', 'scrubbed clean'). Step 4's vocabulary check on 'privatised' shows the difference is public-facing, exactly what D denies.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-034.technique: When a passage CONTRASTS two periods, the right option will mark the SHIFT, not the continuity. Strong contrast verbs ('scrubbed', 'transformed', 'idealised') signal where the shift sits.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: The Moral Climates passage says George III's 1787 Proclamation 'led the charge' against the prudery shift, exhorting against pornography and becoming the basis of the 1857 Obscene Publications Act. It was the OPENING SHOT of a reaction against 18th-century openness — answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about George III's 1787 Proclamation. 'Implied' means: the answer is not stated as a direct definition; you need to read how the Proclamation FUNCTIONS in the passage's narrative. Where does it sit in the moral-shift story?

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: The relevant sentences: 'It is tempting to blame these changes on the prudery of an establishment desperate to re-assert control. King George III led the charge with his 1787 Proclamation for the Encouragement of Piety and Virtue, and for the Preventing and Punishing of Vice, Profaneness and Immorality — an exhortation against pornography that later became the 1857 Obscene Publications Act.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The author has just described the Georgian world as 'transgressive' and is now narrating a swing AWAY from that openness. George III 'led the charge' on this reversal — his Proclamation was the FIRST big official push, and it had real long-term consequences (it grew into a major 1857 law). So it was an early, deliberate reaction against the openness of his own century.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Proclamation' = a formal public order from the monarch. 'Exhortation' = a strong, urgent call to action. 'Led the charge' = was the first or principal driver of a movement. 'Prudery' = excessive or affected concern with proper, modest behaviour. The cluster signals: this was the START of an organised, long-lasting moral counter-movement.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says it was intended to improve the morals of the ROYAL FAMILY itself — there is no claim about the royal family in the passage. B says it was an 'isolated effort with little long-term significance' — the OPPOSITE; the passage says it 'later became the 1857 Obscene Publications Act'. C says it 'united bourgeois and working-class people AGAINST the King' — no such claim; the passage describes it as a King-led elite-down initiative. D says it 'marked a reaction against the moral climate of 18th-century England' — matches 'led the charge' against the 'transgressive' 18th-century environment.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The Proclamation is described as the opening official move in a backlash against the very Georgian openness the previous paragraphs described — exactly what 'reaction against the moral climate of 18th-century England' captures.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'Proclamation' and assume a royal family is policing its own conduct.
distractors.why_wrong: The Proclamation is aimed at vice, profaneness, and immorality in the country at large; the passage frames it as a national moral campaign, not a palace memo. Step 2's quote shows it is targeted at public life.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a 1787 single document is to view it as a historical curiosity.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly traces a direct line from the Proclamation to the 1857 Obscene Publications Act — 70 years of impact. Step 3's paraphrase rules out 'isolated' or 'little long-term significance'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'King' and imagine class conflict between a monarch and his people.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes the Proclamation as part of an ESTABLISHMENT push downward — not as something that united classes against the King. Step 5 shows there is no anti-King coalition mentioned.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-035.technique: On 'what is implied' items, locate the sentence that places the topic IN THE NARRATIVE ARC the author is building. Phrases like 'led the charge', 'opened the way', 'marked the turn' tell you the topic functions as a beginning or pivot — and the right option will name that role.
host-2016-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: An 'implied' question is not a vocabulary lookup of a single line; it asks how the item FUNCTIONS in the paragraph's argument. Locate the surrounding rhetorical move (here: 'the establishment desperate to re-assert control') before matching options.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: The Moral Climates passage says of the new middle class: 'The new middle class saw a link between economics and morality that the Georgian aristocracy had not. They believed that hard work and abstinence could improve people, whereas decadence fostered indolence, disease and poverty.' Their interest in moral matters was framed in economic terms — answer B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what we are told about the 'new middle class' in 19th-century England. Find the cluster of sentences that DESCRIBE that class specifically, then match the option that best characterises their stance — not just one detail.

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: Key sentences: 'The new middle class saw a link between economics and morality that the Georgian aristocracy had not. They believed that hard work and abstinence could improve people, whereas decadence fostered indolence, disease and poverty.' Plus the asylums for 'fallen women' that 'attested to a popular new sense of civic virtue'.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The middle class connected being good with being productive. They thought moral discipline (work, restraint) made people richer and healthier, while immorality (decadence) made people poor and sick. So when they pushed for social and moral reform, the logic underneath was economic: morality pays.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Abstinence' = refraining from indulgence (drinking, sex, excess). 'Decadence' = self-indulgent moral decline. 'Indolence' = laziness. 'Civic virtue' = moral commitment to the common good. 'Proto-feminist' = an early form of feminism. The cluster shows a class that thinks morality and economics are two sides of the same coin.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says their only real interest was making as much money as possible — too narrow; the passage shows they cared genuinely about moral improvement, not just profit. B says they 'concerned themselves with social and moral matters from an economic perspective' — matches 'saw a link between economics and morality' precisely. C says their LACK of social conscience paved the way for prostitution — the OPPOSITE; they built 300 asylums to rescue prostitutes. D says they were pessimistic about reform — also the opposite; they 'believed that hard work and abstinence could improve people'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The new middle class engaged with moral questions through an economic lens — work, abstinence, productivity, all as ways to make society better and richer at the same time.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'capitalism' and 'middle class' and assume the passage paints them as purely money-minded.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says 'we should not dismiss a complex and genuine desire to improve' — Step 2's locate step shows their interest in morality was real, not a cover for profit-seeking.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'prostitution' as a social problem and assume the passage blames the middle class for failing the poor.
distractors.why_wrong: The asylums for 'fallen women' were a middle-class initiative TO HELP prostitutes — that's evidence of social conscience, not the absence of it. Step 4's 'civic virtue' vocabulary check rules C out.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'Christian capitalism' is to imagine a grim doctrinal view of human nature.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says they 'believed that hard work and abstinence COULD IMPROVE people' — that's optimism about reform, not pessimism. Step 3's paraphrase puts the middle class on the hopeful side.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-036.technique: On 'what are we told about X' items, the right option will paraphrase the EXPLANATION the passage gives for X's behaviour — not just label the behaviour itself. Watch for sentences with 'saw a link between', 'believed that', 'because' — they tell you the underlying logic.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The last paragraph closes 'liberals cannot rest on the assumption that liberty always expands over time. Calamity and change can cause it to contract in the blink of an eye.' The general point is that hard-won tolerance can vanish quickly — answer C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for 'the general point, emerging in the last paragraph' of the writer's account. So focus on the FINAL paragraph — the author's wrap-up — and ask what lesson the whole historical sketch is being put in service of.

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: The final paragraph: 'The past is littered with examples of cultural reaction... in the case of the Victorian reaction there are certain parallels to today. The credit crunch has obtained a moral dimension... we might see the re-emergence of corresponding values of piety and sexual restraint. Whatever happens, liberals cannot rest on the assumption that liberty always expands over time. Calamity and change can cause it to contract in the blink of an eye.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: History shows liberty can SWING BACK — Iran, France, Victorian England all saw open eras reverse into stricter ones. Today's economic crisis could trigger the same reversal. So the warning is: don't assume freedoms are safe just because we have them now; calamity can undo them very quickly. The lesson is about FRAGILITY, not progress.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Cultural reaction' = a backlash that swings society back to more conservative norms. 'In the blink of an eye' = very quickly, almost overnight. 'Hard-won' (in option C) = achieved with great difficulty. 'Tolerance' = acceptance of behaviours and identities one might not share. The cluster: liberty is reversible, and reversal happens fast.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says the point is to show how SPECIAL conditions were in England — too narrow; the writer explicitly broadens to Iran, France, and the present-day West. B says it argues FOR the necessity of moral reform today — the passage WARNS about that prospect, it doesn't advocate it. C says it reminds us that 'hard-won tolerance may disappear more or less overnight' — matches 'in the blink of an eye' and 'cannot rest on the assumption' almost word-for-word. D says it demonstrates the IRRELEVANCE of economics to social issues — the OPPOSITE; the credit-crunch parallel is central to the closing argument.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The whole historical sketch — Georgians, Victorians, Iran, France — is in service of one closing warning: liberty is fragile, and we shouldn't assume it always grows.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read a long England-focused history piece as primarily ABOUT England.
distractors.why_wrong: The final paragraph EXPLICITLY widens the lens with Iran, France, and contemporary politics. Step 2's locate step shows the writer treats England as one example among several, not as a special case.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'piety and sexual restraint' in the last paragraph and read it as the writer endorsing a return to those values.
distractors.why_wrong: The writer uses 'we might see' (a prediction-warning) and addresses 'liberals' who shouldn't be complacent — clearly the warning is FROM a liberal vantage point ABOUT a possible loss. Step 3's paraphrase shows the tone is alarmed, not advocating.

distractors.why_tempting: If you skim past 'credit crunch' and 'economic order', you might think the writer is dismissing economics.
distractors.why_wrong: The economic angle IS the connective tissue of the closing argument — economic crisis triggers moral reaction. Step 5 shows the passage treats economics as deeply relevant, not irrelevant.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-037.technique: On 'general point' items about a CLOSING paragraph, ignore the historical examples and zero in on the author's wrap-up sentences ('Whatever happens...', 'cannot rest on...'). That's where the thesis lives.
host-2016-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: Long historical passages with vivid examples bait you toward an option that names the examples (Victorian, English, royal). The thesis is usually a single abstract lesson DRAWN from the examples — find the lesson, not the source material.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The Ecology passage says of Arne Naess's deep ecology: 'His concept entered the mainstream green movement in the 1980s.' So deep ecology is now part of the philosophy of the green movement — answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is said 'in relation to ecology' — a broad question that wants you to summarise the passage's main factual claim about ecology, especially the one the author closes on. Find the sentence that has the most reach.

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: Key sentences: 'Arne Naess was Norway's best-known philosopher, whose concept of deep ecology enriched and divided the environmental movement.' And the closer: 'His concept entered the mainstream green movement in the 1980s.' The contrast: deep ecology (every part of nature has equal value) vs shallow ecology (problems solvable within capitalism).

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Naess proposed 'deep ecology': nature has value beyond human use, and ecological problems can't be solved by tweaking the existing economy. This idea joined the mainstream green movement in the 1980s. So deep ecology is now part of how environmentalists think — it became part of the movement's philosophy.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Deep ecology' = the view that nature has intrinsic value, not just instrumental value to humans. 'Shallow ecology' = the view that environmental problems can be solved without changing the economic system. 'Mainstream' = the dominant, widely accepted version of something. 'Enriched and divided' = both deepened and split the movement.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says Naess was 'instrumental in the CREATION of the green movement' — too strong; the passage says his concept ENTERED the existing movement in the 1980s, not that he founded it. B says there is ONE MAIN STRAND of ecological understanding — the OPPOSITE; the passage names two (deep and shallow). C says Naess encouraged ecologically SOUND LIVES — the passage describes his philosophy, not personal lifestyle advice. D says deep ecology is now part of the philosophy of the green movement — matches 'His concept entered the mainstream green movement in the 1980s'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The passage's central factual claim is that Naess's deep ecology made it INTO mainstream green thought — exactly what 'now part of the philosophy of the green movement' captures.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'Norway's best-known philosopher' alongside the green movement and slot Naess in as a founder.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says his concept ENTERED the green movement in the 1980s — it pre-existed him. Step 5 shows the verb 'entered' rules out 'instrumental in the creation'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct after reading about a single theorist is to assume his view IS the ecological view.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly contrasts deep ecology with shallow ecology — two strands, not one. Step 2's locate step shows Naess's concept 'divided' the movement, which means multiple strands.

distractors.why_tempting: Many associate environmental philosophy with personal lifestyle prescriptions (recycle, reduce).
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes Naess's PHILOSOPHICAL claims about nature's value, not any personal-conduct exhortation. Step 3's paraphrase shows the level of the argument is theoretical.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-038.technique: On 'what is said in relation to X' items, find the sentence that BROADENS from the specific topic to its place in the wider field. Verbs like 'entered the mainstream', 'became part of', 'transformed' flag the option-level claim.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The Groups passage says: 'a group's collective intelligence is affected not by individual intelligence but by social sensitivity and the ability to make inferences — skills women are more likely to possess than men.' The headline is that smart groups have a mix of abilities (especially women's social ones) — answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the 'main point' of the Groups paragraph. Main-point questions want the one idea that holds the paragraph together. Read the whole short paragraph and ask: what's the headline finding?

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: The whole paragraph is the evidence: 'Just because a group is full of intelligent individuals does not mean the group as a whole will necessarily be smart. But having more women does... a group's collective intelligence is affected not by individual intelligence but by social sensitivity and the ability to make inferences — skills women are more likely to possess than men.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: A clever group isn't just a bag of clever individuals. What makes the group smart is the SOCIAL skills inside it — reading other people, making inferences — and women tend to bring more of those skills. So group intelligence depends on the MIX of abilities, not on raw individual smarts.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Collective intelligence' = the smartness of a group as a whole, not the average of its members. 'Social sensitivity' = ability to read social cues, recognise emotions. 'Inferences' = conclusions drawn from indirect evidence. 'Mixed abilities' (option D) = a range of different skills coexisting in the group.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says women in general are MORE INTELLIGENT than men — overreach; the passage says women are more likely to have SPECIFIC SOCIAL SKILLS, not greater general intelligence. B says successful groups require intelligent men — the passage doesn't claim this; men's intelligence isn't named as a requirement. C says women appreciate group work BETTER than men — about preferences, not about contribution; not in the passage. D says intelligent groups consist of people with MIXED ABILITIES — matches 'social sensitivity AND ability to make inferences', plus the contrast with individual IQ.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The paragraph's headline is that group smartness comes from a MIX of skills (social and inferential), not from any single dimension — and that's exactly what 'mixed abilities' captures.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'having more women does' make groups smarter and conclude women are generally smarter.
distractors.why_wrong: The very next clause names SPECIFIC skills (social sensitivity, inference-making) — not general intelligence. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the claim local to those skills.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a 'success' framing is to fill in both genders' contributions symmetrically.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't say intelligent men are a requirement — it says individual intelligence isn't the driver at all. Step 2's locate step shows the contrast is individual vs collective, not men vs women on intelligence.

distractors.why_tempting: Many associate gender and group work with preference rather than performance.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage is about who CONTRIBUTES TO making a group smart, not who PREFERS or appreciates group work. Step 5 shows the option moves the topic from contribution to taste.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-039.technique: On main-point questions, find the sentence that names the MECHANISM or CAUSE — here 'affected not by individual intelligence but by social sensitivity and inference'. The correct option will name the mechanism in different words.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The Iatrogenic Deaths passage acknowledges that medical interventions cause harm but also says treatments like chemotherapy and antibiotics 'do not do more harm than good overall' and that research into them will both reveal harms AND lead to better treatments. The implication: some fatal side effects come with even necessary treatments — answer C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what CAN BE CONCLUDED from the text. 'Conclude' means: draw a defensible inference from what the passage says. The right option will be supported by the passage's overall stance, not just one sentence.

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: Key sentences: 'Many medical interventions turn out to have unintended and negative consequences... None of this suggests that treatments such as chemotherapy, painkillers or antibiotics do more harm than good overall. And the research that reveals their effects will help us to develop still more effective treatments. But it does highlight the need to think broadly about iatrogenic deaths.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Medicine saves lives but also causes some deaths through errors, drug reactions, and infections. Even valuable treatments (chemo, antibiotics) carry some inevitable downsides. The author doesn't want to give them up — just wants people to face that some harm is built in. So the conclusion is: some fatal consequences come along with useful medicine, and we should accept and address that.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Iatrogenic' = caused by medical treatment itself (from Greek 'iatros' = healer). 'Adverse drug reaction' = a harmful response to a medicine. 'Inevitable' (option C) = unavoidable, certain to happen. 'Alleviate suffering' = reduce pain. The cluster shows the passage accepts BOTH the harm and the necessity of these treatments.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says some well-known treatments should NEVER have been developed — too strong; the passage explicitly defends chemo, painkillers, antibiotics as 'not doing more harm than good overall'. B says iatrogenic deaths will become EVEN MORE common in the future — the passage doesn't predict this; it just says current levels need thinking about. C says some fatal consequences of medical treatments are PROBABLY INEVITABLE — matches the acceptance tone ('None of this suggests... do more harm than good overall' implies the harms are part of the package). D says iatrogenic deaths can EASILY be avoided with more solid research — the OPPOSITE; the passage says research will reveal effects, not eliminate them.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The passage accepts that valuable treatments carry built-in risks — research will sharpen our understanding but won't make harms vanish. That's exactly the 'probably inevitable' framing.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'third leading cause of death' as a damning indictment of modern medicine and conclude some treatments shouldn't exist.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly defends those treatments: 'None of this suggests that... chemotherapy, painkillers or antibiotics do more harm than good overall.' Step 2's locate step pins the author's position to support, not condemnation.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the rule 'more treatments → more side effects' as a trend line, B feels like a natural projection.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't project a future trend — it analyses the present situation. Step 3's paraphrase has no forecast in it; the call is for thinking broadly NOW, not predicting growth.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'highlight the need to think broadly' is to read it as a call to fix the problem entirely with better research.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says research helps develop BETTER treatments — not that it ELIMINATES iatrogenic deaths. Step 5 shows the verb 'easily avoided' is a much stronger claim than the passage's hedged language supports.

host-2016-verb1-ELF-040.technique: On 'what can be concluded' items, look for the option that matches the AUTHOR'S OWN STANCE — accepting trade-offs, hedging, or balancing. Strong-claim options (NEVER, ALWAYS, EASILY) are almost always traps when the passage uses careful, balanced language.
host-2016-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: Conclude-from-text questions reward the option that the author would AGREE with based on the passage's overall tone — not the option that fixes the problem in the most ambitious way. Strong-fix language ('easily avoided', 'never developed') usually overshoots the author's measured position.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: Gap 31 sits in 'the Danish government also wanted to _____ revenue'. The verb pairs with 'revenue', and the standard English collocation is 'raise revenue' (= generate, increase). The next sentence confirms: 'the tax did bring in $216 million'. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a cloze gap where the missing word is a VERB that takes 'revenue' as its object. The four options are all verbs in the same form, so the question is asking: which verb collocates naturally with 'revenue' AND fits the policy context?

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: 'By introducing its fat tax in 2011, which was dropped after only a year, the Danish government also wanted to _____ revenue, reduce costs associated with obesity-related diseases, and increase health and longevity.' The verb sits in a list of three policy GOALS for the tax. Next sentence: 'the tax did bring in $216 million'.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The Danish government wanted MORE revenue from the tax — that's the whole point of imposing it as a money-raising measure. So the verb needs to mean 'generate', 'increase', or 'bring in' revenue. The verb is paired in the same sentence with 'reduce costs' and 'increase health', which sets the pattern: a verb of bringing something UP.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Cut revenue' = reduce the amount of revenue. 'Spread revenue' = distribute revenue across some area (unusual, and not what governments DO with their own revenue). 'Raise revenue' = generate or increase revenue — this is the fixed English idiom in policy contexts. 'End revenue' = stop revenue (the opposite of what a tax does).

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Test each option in the sentence. A 'wanted to CUT revenue' — opposite of imposing a tax; doesn't fit. B 'wanted to SPREAD revenue' — possible but uncollocated; governments don't 'spread' their own incoming revenue. C 'wanted to RAISE revenue' — the standard idiom, matches 'bring in $216 million'. D 'wanted to END revenue' — opposite again, and 'end revenue' is not idiomatic.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. 'Raise revenue' is the fixed English collocation for generating tax income, and it lines up with the very next sentence that confirms the tax brought in $216 million.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to misread the surrounding context as criticism of the tax and pair 'cut' with the tax's failure.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Cut revenue' is the opposite of what a NEW tax is designed to do. Step 3's relationship check sets the direction as UP, not down.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'spread' as a neutral-sounding verb and assume it must work somewhere.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Spread revenue' isn't an English collocation for income generation — you might 'spread costs' across years, but governments don't 'spread' incoming revenue. Step 4's vocabulary check rules it out.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct may be to associate 'end' with 'finished after a year' from the same sentence.
distractors.why_wrong: 'End revenue' is not idiomatic; you END a tax or a program, not the revenue itself. Step 5's plug-and-test shows the sentence becomes nonsensical with 'end'.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-031.technique: On cloze verb gaps, identify the OBJECT first, then ask which verb forms a fixed English collocation with that object. 'Raise revenue', 'reduce costs', 'cut taxes', 'meet demand' — these are unbreakable pairs that ELF tests over and over.
host-2016-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: Cloze options often include verbs that sound vaguely policy-adjacent ('spread', 'end') but aren't standard collocations. When in doubt, ask: would a fluent English newspaper writer actually use this verb-object pair?

host-2016-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: Gap 32 sits between 'Business groups insist that the tax had no effect on the amount of fat that Danes ate' and 'economists at the University of Copenhagen say Danish fat consumption fell by 10 to 20 per cent'. The two claims oppose each other, so the connector signals contrast — 'In contrast'. Answer A.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This cloze gap holds a DISCOURSE MARKER — a connector between two sentences. The four options are all sentence-starter phrases that signal different relationships (contrast, example, consequence, reference). Identify the relationship between the two surrounding sentences, then pick the connector that fits.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Sentence A: 'Business groups insist that the tax had no effect on the amount of fat that Danes ate.' Then gap. Sentence B: 'economists at the University of Copenhagen say Danish fat consumption fell by 10 to 20 per cent in the first three months after the tax went into effect.' One says 'no effect', the other says 'fell by 10-20%'. They directly oppose each other.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Two different sources give two different findings — 'no effect' from business groups vs '10-20% fall' from economists. That's a CONTRAST: the second sentence sets up the opposite view from the first. So the connector needs to flag opposition.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'In contrast' = on the opposite side; introduces an opposing view. 'For example' = introduces an instance of the same idea. 'In consequence' = as a result; signals cause and effect. 'For reference' = for the sake of comparison or pointing to something — not idiomatic as a sentence-starter at all. The cluster: only 'In contrast' fits a sharp opposition.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Test each option. A 'In contrast' — yes; business groups say no effect, economists say 10-20% drop; perfect opposition. B 'For example' — no; the economists' finding isn't an EXAMPLE of business groups' claim, it CONTRADICTS it. C 'In consequence' — no; the economists' study isn't a result of the business groups' statement; they're independent claims. D 'For reference' — no; this isn't a sentence connector in normal English, and there's no comparative anchor being offered.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The two sentences carry directly opposing findings about the tax's effect on fat consumption — 'In contrast' is the only connector that names the relationship between them.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'fell by 10-20%' as a specific number and assume the second sentence ILLUSTRATES the first.
distractors.why_wrong: An example would SUPPORT the first claim; here the second sentence undermines it. Step 3's relationship check shows opposition, not illustration.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'after the tax went into effect' and read the 10-20% fall as a CONSEQUENCE of the tax.
distractors.why_wrong: Even if the fall is a consequence of the tax, the connector links the SECOND sentence to the FIRST sentence — and business groups' insistence isn't the cause of the economists' study. Step 5 separates sentence-level connection from real-world causation.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on an unfamiliar discourse marker is to assume it works in some niche academic register.
distractors.why_wrong: 'For reference' is not an English sentence-connector and would sound jarring in a newspaper article. Step 4's vocabulary check rules it out as ungrammatical in this position.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-032.technique: On cloze discourse markers, name the relationship between the two surrounding sentences in one word — contrast, example, cause, conclusion, addition. Then pick the connector that labels that relationship. 'In contrast' specifically demands two opposing claims on either side.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: Gap 33 is in 'Because the different fatty acids _____ in their risk, imposing a single tax on them... is difficult to support scientifically.' The author's point is that fatty acids carry DIFFERENT risks, so the verb means 'differ' — answer D ('vary').

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This cloze gap is a VERB following 'different fatty acids' as subject and taking 'in their risk' as the prepositional complement. The four options are all verbs but with very different meanings. Read the surrounding logic to see what claim the verb has to carry.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: 'All food fats are a mix of unsaturated and saturated fatty acids. Saturated fats raise the risk of coronary heart disease, although not by much. Trans fats... are a greater risk factor. Because the different fatty acids _____ in their risk, imposing a single tax on them as if they are indistinguishable is difficult to support scientifically.'

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The paragraph has just listed how DIFFERENT types of fat (saturated, trans, unsaturated) carry DIFFERENT levels of risk — saturated 'not by much', trans 'a greater risk factor'. The 'because' clause then sums this up: the fatty acids ARE NOT THE SAME in their risk. So the verb needs to mean 'differ' or 'are not uniform'.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Decrease in their risk' = the risk goes down (a directional change, not a comparison between types). 'Progress in their risk' = uncollocated; not standard English here. 'Advance in their risk' = also uncollocated, and would suggest forward motion, not difference. 'Vary in their risk' = differ from one to another, are not uniform — exactly what the paragraph has shown. The cluster: only 'vary' captures inter-type difference.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Test each option. A 'decrease in their risk' — describes a downward trend, not differences between types; doesn't fit the 'because' logic. B 'progress in their risk' — not idiomatic English; risk doesn't 'progress'. C 'advance in their risk' — same problem; 'advance' implies forward movement, not variability. D 'vary in their risk' — captures the differences between saturated, trans, and unsaturated; supports the 'because... a single tax is difficult to justify' argument.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. 'Vary' is the English verb for 'differ from one type to another', and the paragraph's whole point is that fats AREN'T uniform in their health impact — so taxing them all the same is scientifically shaky.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'risk' and assume the verb names a directional change in that risk.
distractors.why_wrong: The argument requires DIFFERENCE between types, not a downward trend in any single type. Step 3's relationship check shows the comparison is across saturated/trans/unsaturated, not over time.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'progress' as a positive-sounding verb and don't notice it doesn't pair with 'risk'.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Progress in their risk' is not an English collocation; risk doesn't 'progress' as a meaningful claim. Step 4's vocabulary check rules it out.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'advance' as a synonym for 'progress', C looks interchangeable with B.
distractors.why_wrong: Same problem as B — risk doesn't 'advance' in standard English. Step 5's plug-and-test shows both progress/advance fail because neither matches the contrast structure the paragraph builds.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-033.technique: On cloze verb gaps inside a 'because' clause, ask: what claim does the 'because' have to support? Then pick the verb that makes that claim true. Here the conclusion 'a single tax is difficult to support' requires the premise 'they're not all the same' — i.e. they vary.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: Gap 34 sits between 'anti-obesity tax measures... have tended to avoid targeting broad nutrient groups' and 'they focus on processed foods, fast food or sugary drinks'. The second sentence describes what other countries do INSTEAD of broad targeting — answer C ('Instead').

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This cloze gap holds a DISCOURSE MARKER between two sentences describing what other countries do (or don't do) with food taxes. The four options are all sentence-starter phrases. Name the relationship between 'avoid X' and 'focus on Y' to pick the right connector.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Sentence A: 'For these reasons, anti-obesity tax measures in other countries have tended to avoid targeting broad nutrient groups.' Then gap. Sentence B: 'they focus on processed foods, fast food or sugary drinks – all major sources of calories.' Sentence A says they AVOID one approach; Sentence B says they CHOOSE a different approach.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The pair forms an X-NOT-Y / Y-INSTEAD structure: 'we don't do A; we do B'. The second sentence offers a REPLACEMENT for the first sentence's rejected approach. So the connector signals substitution: 'instead of A, B'.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'However' = signals contrast or qualification; works when the second sentence pushes back. 'Unfortunately' = signals regret or undesirable outcome. 'Instead' = in place of the previous option; signals substitution. 'Subsequently' = afterwards, signals time order. The cluster: only 'Instead' carries the 'replacement' relationship.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Test each option. A 'However' — too soft; the second sentence doesn't qualify the first, it OFFERS the alternative. B 'Unfortunately' — wrong tone; there's no regret in the second sentence, it describes a 'more promising' strategy. C 'Instead' — yes; 'they avoid broad nutrients; instead, they focus on processed foods' reads cleanly. D 'Subsequently' — wrong relationship; there's no time order, just substitution.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The two sentences set up a clean 'not A, but B' pair — and 'Instead' is the standard English connector for that substitution structure.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to default to 'However' as a generic discourse marker whenever a new sentence introduces a different angle.
distractors.why_wrong: 'However' implies that the second sentence pushes BACK against the first; here the second sentence FOLLOWS NATURALLY from the first as the alternative approach. Step 3 shows the relationship is substitution, not qualification.

distractors.why_tempting: Many associate food-tax discussions with disappointment about public health and reach for 'Unfortunately' as a tonal fit.
distractors.why_wrong: The second sentence describes a 'more promising strategy' — that's optimistic, not unfortunate. Step 4's vocabulary check shows the tone doesn't fit.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on policy narratives is to read events in time order; 'subsequently' feels like a generic 'then'.
distractors.why_wrong: The two sentences describe a CHOICE, not a sequence of events. Step 5's plug-and-test shows 'subsequently' would suggest the substitution happened later, which isn't the structure here.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-034.technique: On cloze discourse markers, look for the 'NOT A, BUT B' shape in adjacent sentences. When one sentence describes what is AVOIDED and the next describes what is CHOSEN, the connector is 'Instead' almost without exception.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: Gap 35 sits in 'That they have a role in addressing the health problems caused by obesity is beyond _____.' The fixed English idiom is 'beyond debate' = 'not open to question'. Answer B.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This cloze gap holds a NOUN following the preposition 'beyond'. The four options are all nouns, but only one forms a fixed English idiom with 'beyond' that means 'not in question'. Read for context, then test each option against the idiom check.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: 'What else should governments be doing? That they have a role in addressing the health problems caused by obesity is beyond _____, not least because they bear much of the cost of dealing with such problems.' The author is moving past the QUESTION of whether governments should act, on the way to discussing HOW — the role itself is established.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The author wants to say: 'the question of whether governments should act is settled — let's move on'. The noun needs to mean 'something open to question or argument', so that 'beyond [it]' means 'no longer open to question'. So we need a noun naming dispute, argument, or doubt.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Beyond reason' = irrational, crazy (negative connotation). 'Beyond debate' = no longer open to argument; settled. 'Beyond relevance' = uncollocated; we say 'no longer relevant', not 'beyond relevance'. 'Beyond belief' = unbelievable, astonishing. Of the four, only 'beyond debate' carries the 'no-longer-in-question' meaning.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Test each option. A 'beyond reason' — would mean governments' role is IRRATIONAL; the opposite of what the author argues. B 'beyond debate' — fits exactly; the author moves past the WHETHER question to HOW. C 'beyond relevance' — not an English idiom; doesn't parse. D 'beyond belief' — would mean governments' role is UNBELIEVABLE, which makes no sense in the policy argument.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. 'Beyond debate' is the fixed English idiom for 'settled, not open to question' — and that's exactly the move the author makes before turning to the 'But how?' question that closes the paragraph.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to associate 'beyond reason' with strong-claim phrasing and assume it could mean 'beyond question'.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Beyond reason' means CRAZY or IRRATIONAL — strongly negative. Step 4's vocabulary check shows the idiom carries the opposite connotation to what the author needs.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'relevance' as a general-purpose policy word and assume it must work somewhere.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Beyond relevance' is not an English collocation; the standard phrasing would be 'no longer relevant'. Step 4's vocabulary check rules it out.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'beyond X' constructions might be the more familiar 'beyond belief' (= astonishing).
distractors.why_wrong: 'Beyond belief' means unbelievable in a HYPERBOLIC sense, not 'no longer open to question'. Step 5's plug-and-test shows the sentence becomes nonsensical with that meaning.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-035.technique: On cloze noun gaps after 'beyond X', think of the fixed English idiom set: 'beyond doubt', 'beyond debate', 'beyond question', 'beyond compare', 'beyond belief', 'beyond reason'. Only some of those mean 'settled' — match the meaning the surrounding argument needs before picking.
host-2016-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: Cloze noun options often share a vague 'argument' field (reason, relevance, belief, debate) but only one forms the standard idiom in the slot. When in doubt, ask: would 'beyond [option]' appear in this exact form in a newspaper?

host-2016-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The opening paragraph of the Vaccine review says 'News of Jenner's stunning achievement led millions throughout Europe to roll up their sleeves' AND that 'not everyone welcomed Jenner's feat. Criticism came quickly, often in apocalyptic terms.' The reception was MIXED — both enthusiastic and hostile. Answer B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what we are told in the OPENING PARAGRAPH. So restrict focus to the first paragraph and gather the cluster of claims about Jenner's discovery and its early reception. The right option will summarise the WHOLE paragraph's tone, not a single sentence.

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: The opening paragraph mentions: Jenner's 1796 smallpox vaccination; 'News of Jenner's stunning achievement led millions throughout Europe to roll up their sleeves'; 'not everyone welcomed Jenner's feat. Criticism came quickly, often in apocalyptic terms'; specific critics named (Malthus, ministers, others worried about poisoning the bloodstream).

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The opening tells two sides at once: millions enthusiastically lined up for vaccination AND vocal critics attacked the idea (overpopulation, religious objections, fear of foreign matter in the body). So the reception was simultaneously warm AND hostile — a mixed picture, not uniform.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Immunized' = made resistant to a disease (here, by vaccination). 'Apocalyptic terms' = framed as catastrophic or world-ending. 'Mixed reception' (option B) = received with both positive and negative responses. 'Stunning achievement' = remarkable accomplishment. The cluster: enthusiasm and hostility coexisted from the start.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says it took a LONG TIME for doctors to put Jenner's ideas into large-scale practice — the opposite; 'millions throughout Europe' rolled up their sleeves rapidly. B says Jenner's discovery was given a MIXED reception — matches both the millions who accepted AND the apocalyptic critics. C says the PUBLIC found it difficult to believe Jenner's claims — partially right about critics, but ignores the millions who DID accept; too one-sided. D says the usefulness was widely doubted in MEDICAL circles — the criticism in the passage comes from an economist (Malthus), ministers, and laymen worried about poisoning, not from doctors as a profession.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The opening paragraph deliberately stages BOTH the enthusiastic uptake AND the apocalyptic backlash — the 'mixed reception' framing is exactly what holds the paragraph together.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to imagine 18th-century medical innovations as slow to spread and project that onto Jenner.
distractors.why_wrong: The opening paragraph explicitly says 'millions throughout Europe' rolled up their sleeves — fast and wide adoption. Step 2's locate step shows the spread was rapid, not slow.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the apocalyptic criticism and read the opening as a story of public disbelief.
distractors.why_wrong: The very same paragraph says millions enthusiastically accepted vaccination — the public was NOT uniformly skeptical. Step 3's paraphrase keeps both sides in view; C drops the enthusiasm half.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on resistance to a new medical idea is to attribute it to medical professionals defending their turf.
distractors.why_wrong: The named critics are an ECONOMIST (Malthus), ministers (religious), and unspecified objectors worried about 'poisonous matter' — not doctors as a group. Step 5 shows the criticism doesn't come from medical circles.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-036.technique: On 'opening paragraph' questions, watch for paragraphs that explicitly stage TWO SIDES — they're designed to test whether you noticed both. 'Yet', 'not everyone', 'however' inside the paragraph are flags that the right option will name a tension or mixed reaction.
host-2016-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: When a paragraph has a dominant tone (here, the enthusiasm of 'stunning achievement, millions rolled up their sleeves'), it's easy to forget the counter-current introduced by 'Yet'. Read the paragraph for BOTH movements before matching.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The Vaccine review says Jenner's vaccine was followed by 'a procession of other vaccines, for rabies, tetanus, yellow fever, diphtheria, and more. A healthier diet, advances in sanitation and surgery, the development of antibiotics and DDT – all combined to increase American life expectancy to 70 years from 47.' The vaccine was ONE of several factors — answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about Jenner's vaccine in relation to the GENERAL HEALTH SITUATION in the US in the last century. So zoom in on the paragraph that links vaccines to broader public health and ask: how is Jenner's contribution positioned?

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: Key sentences: 'Jenner's triumph was followed by a procession of other vaccines, for rabies, tetanus, yellow fever, diphtheria, and more. A healthier diet, advances in sanitation and surgery, the development of antibiotics and DDT — all combined to increase American life expectancy to 70 years from 47 between 1900 and 1955.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Jenner's vaccine kicked off a long line of other vaccines, AND alongside vaccines came better food, cleaner cities, surgery improvements, antibiotics, DDT. All of these together pushed US life expectancy up. So Jenner's smallpox work is ONE strand of a broader fabric — important, but not the only cause of the public-health gains.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'A procession of' = a long sequence of. 'Sanitation' = clean water supply, sewage treatment, public hygiene. 'Life expectancy' = the average age people live to. 'Combined' = worked together. The cluster shows Jenner's vaccine is placed in a LIST of contributing factors, not singled out as the cause.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says its role has been EXAGGERATED — too negative; the passage describes Jenner's work as 'medicine's greatest lifesaver' and treats the vaccine as a genuine contribution. B says it was gradually found effective against a VARIETY of diseases — but Jenner's vaccine targets smallpox specifically; later vaccines for other diseases came from other researchers. C says its use was long resisted by American health AUTHORITIES — the passage describes resistance from the public ('individual responsibility'), not from health authorities. D says it was ONE of several important factors behind health improvements — matches the list 'vaccines... diet... sanitation... surgery... antibiotics... all combined'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The passage carefully lists multiple causes of rising life expectancy and places Jenner's vaccine as the OPENING ENTRY in that list — important, but not solo. 'One of several' is exactly that placement.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'all combined' and conclude that no single contribution was as important as the popular history makes it.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage frames Jenner's work positively ('triumph', 'medicine's greatest lifesaver'); placing him as one of several factors is acknowledgement, not deflation. Step 4's vocabulary check on 'a procession of' rules out exaggeration claims.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'a procession of other vaccines' and merge them all into Jenner's contribution.
distractors.why_wrong: Jenner's vaccine is for smallpox specifically; rabies, tetanus, yellow fever vaccines came from different researchers. Step 2's locate step shows Jenner's role was the smallpox vaccine, not a multi-disease one.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'antivaccine sentiment found fertile soil in the United States' is to read it as authority-level resistance.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage names the source of resistance as 'the ethos of individual responsibility' clashing with public health programs — that's a CULTURAL resistance, not opposition from health authorities. Step 5 shows the actors are the public, not officialdom.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-037.technique: On 'implied about X' items where X appears in a LIST of causes, look for the option that names X as ONE OF SEVERAL — not as solo cause or as failure. Words like 'combined', 'alongside', 'a procession of' flag the list structure.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: The Vaccine review says of the 1950s: 'As late as the 1950s, parents had been encouraged to expose their children to diseases like measles and mumps in order to get them over with before adulthood, when the dangers increased.' So 1950s medicine recognised that some diseases were MORE DANGEROUS for adults than for children — answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what we are told about the 1950s 'from a medical point of view'. Find the paragraph that specifically discusses 1950s medical practice and gather the cluster of claims. The right option will summarise what that paragraph teaches about the era's medical thinking.

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: Key sentences: 'As late as the 1950s, parents had been encouraged to expose their children to diseases like measles and mumps in order to get them over with before adulthood, when the dangers increased. Vaccines for these illnesses have been around since the 1970s. More were on the way and some researchers spoke openly about a future without infectious disease. In our current age of AIDS, one marvels at the arrogance of such a belief.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: In the 1950s, doctors and parents thought it was better to let kids catch measles and mumps young, because the same diseases were worse for adults. So the medical understanding was that childhood diseases became MORE DANGEROUS once people grew up — which made early exposure look medically sensible at the time.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Expose' (children to a disease) = let them catch it deliberately. 'Get them over with' = experience and recover from. 'Dangers increased' (with adulthood) = the diseases became more harmful in adults. The cluster: childhood diseases were treated as relatively safe for children but dangerous for adults — a contrast in risk by age.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says the 1950s had JUSTIFIED optimism about eliminating all infectious disease — the passage says 'one marvels at the arrogance of such a belief' — clearly NOT justified. B says parents were asked to PREVENT their children from getting diseases — the OPPOSITE; the passage says they were encouraged to EXPOSE children. C says it was a period of dangerous outbreaks throughout the US — not what the paragraph claims; outbreaks aren't mentioned. D says there was awareness that some diseases posed a GREATER THREAT TO ADULTS than to children — matches 'before adulthood, when the dangers increased'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The whole logic of the early-exposure recommendation hinges on the medical awareness that measles and mumps were more dangerous in adulthood. That awareness is exactly what D names.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'a future without infectious disease' and assume the optimism was endorsed by the passage.
distractors.why_wrong: The very next sentence calls that belief 'arrogance' — the author dismisses the optimism, not endorses it. Step 2's locate step shows the qualifier 'one marvels at the arrogance' rules out 'justified'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'parents had been encouraged' and assume what follows is a public-health prevention message.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage specifies parents were encouraged to EXPOSE children, not protect them. Step 3's paraphrase pins the policy as deliberate exposure, the opposite of B.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'measles and mumps' is to imagine the era as full of outbreaks before vaccines existed.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph doesn't describe outbreaks; it describes a deliberate parental strategy. Step 5 shows C imports an idea (large-scale outbreaks) that the paragraph never makes.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-038.technique: On 'what are we told about year X' items, locate the paragraph that anchors on that year and read it as a unit. The right option will paraphrase the underlying medical or social LOGIC of the era, not just one phrase.
host-2016-verb2-ELF-038.pitfall: Vivid claims like 'a future without infectious disease' bait you toward an option that endorses optimism. The author's immediate qualifier ('one marvels at the arrogance') flips the framing — read the WHOLE sentence pair before choosing.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-039.solution_path: The Vaccine review says Allen sees the antivaccinist leadership as 'composed of countercultural types who view life through the prism of conspiracy theory'. Allen is therefore SUSPICIOUS of the arguments these skeptics make — answer B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for 'Arthur Allen's attitude to vaccination skeptics'. Find the passage describing Allen's view of the antivaccine movement specifically, then choose the option that names his stance toward the SKEPTICS' arguments, not toward parents' fears in general.

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: Key sentences: 'Allen is sympathetic to parental fears regarding the dangers of various vaccines, though he remains skeptical that scientific studies of these dangers will open many minds. At this point, he writes, much of the "antivaccinist" leadership is composed of countercultural types who view life through the prism of conspiracy theory: the government lies, the drug companies are evil, the medical profession is corrupt; trust the Internet instead. A fair number oppose traditional medicine in favor of homeopathy, believing that vaccines weaken the immune system and that sickness is a natural part of life.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Allen splits the population into two groups. Ordinary worried parents — he's sympathetic. The MOVEMENT LEADERSHIP — he describes them in negative terms: conspiracy theorists, anti-establishment by reflex, preferring homeopathy and Internet rumours to real medicine. So Allen is suspicious of the leaders' arguments, even while he's gentle on individual parents.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Antivaccinist' = someone who opposes vaccination. 'Countercultural' = rejecting mainstream values and institutions. 'Prism of conspiracy theory' = a worldview that sees plots everywhere. 'Suspicious of' (option B) = doubtful, distrustful. 'Homeopathy' = an alternative medicine system using highly diluted substances. The cluster: Allen treats the leaders' worldview as compromised by ideology, hence not credible.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says Allen agrees research into vaccine effects shouldn't be trusted — the OPPOSITE; Allen trusts the research, he just doubts it will change skeptics' minds. B says he is SUSPICIOUS of antivaccinist arguments — matches the conspiracy-theory and homeopathy framing. C says he fails to UNDERSTAND parental worry — the OPPOSITE; he is explicitly 'sympathetic to parental fears'. D says he believes personal views deserve equal weight to scientific evidence — the OPPOSITE of his pro-research stance.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. Allen separates parents (worth sympathy) from the antivaccinist LEADERSHIP (driven by conspiracy theory and ideology). His attitude to the leaders' arguments — those are what the question targets — is suspicion.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'skeptical that scientific studies... will open many minds' as Allen himself being skeptical of those studies.
distractors.why_wrong: He's skeptical about the studies' ABILITY TO CONVINCE antivaccinists — not skeptical of the studies themselves. Step 3's paraphrase puts Allen on the pro-research side.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the harsh framing of conspiracy theorists and assume Allen is dismissive of all parental worry.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says Allen is 'sympathetic to parental fears' — he understands parents perfectly well. Step 2's locate step makes this distinction explicit.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember Allen as 'fair-minded' and 'balanced' from the review's framing, D might look like the neutral middle ground.
distractors.why_wrong: Allen is described as fair-minded but ALSO as defending science against conspiracy thinking — he doesn't equate personal opinion with scientific evidence. Step 5 shows D would put him in a relativist position the passage rules out.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-039.technique: On 'attitude to X' items, watch for passages that SPLIT the topic into subgroups (parents vs leaders, public vs experts, theory vs practice). The right option targets the precise subgroup named in the prompt, not the general topic.
host-2016-verb2-ELF-039.pitfall: The same author can hold DIFFERENT attitudes toward different parts of a movement (sympathy for parents, suspicion of leaders). Don't collapse the subgroups into one stance — pick the option that targets the exact group in the question.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-040.solution_path: The Vaccine review closes 'For Allen, a reversal of these trends will require something long overdue: a frank national discussion about the risks and benefits of vaccination.' Allen wants the US to face the current situation with serious consideration of pros and cons — answer C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for Allen's view of the PRESENT situation concerning vaccination in the US. Find the closing paragraph where Allen's prescription for the present moment is laid out, and match the option that names what he calls for.

steps.title: Locate in the passage
steps.text: Key sentences: 'As more children go unvaccinated in the United States, there has been a rise in vaccine-preventable diseases. Meanwhile, fewer pharmaceutical companies are now producing vaccines, citing the high cost of testing, diminishing markets and a fear of litigation. For Allen, a reversal of these trends will require something long overdue: a frank national discussion about the risks and benefits of vaccination.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Allen sees two current trends he wants reversed: fewer kids getting vaccinated (so vaccine-preventable diseases are coming back), and fewer companies making vaccines (because of costs, smaller markets, lawsuit risk). His prescription: a serious, honest public conversation that weighs the risks AND the benefits of vaccination. So he wants the country to face the trade-offs squarely.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Long overdue' = should have happened long ago. 'Frank national discussion' = an honest, open public conversation. 'Pros and cons' (option C) = arguments for and against — i.e., risks and benefits. 'Reversal of these trends' = turning around the current direction. The cluster: Allen wants public engagement, not retreat.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says 20th-century vaccination programmes have made it LESS NECESSARY to vaccinate today's children — the OPPOSITE; Allen is alarmed that fewer kids being vaccinated is bringing diseases back. B says it's DOUBTFUL whether overall vaccination effects are positive — Allen frames vaccination as 'medicine's greatest lifesaver'; he doesn't doubt the positive overall effect. C says recent developments will have to be met by SERIOUS CONSIDERATION OF PROS AND CONS — matches 'a frank national discussion about the risks and benefits' precisely. D says the discovery of new diseases without vaccines is UNAVOIDABLE — there's no claim about new diseases in the passage; that's not the present-situation issue.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. Allen's prescription — a frank discussion of risks AND benefits — is exactly the 'serious consideration of pros and cons' that C names. That's how he wants the country to handle the current trends.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'most parents today are blissfully unaware of the diseases their children are inoculated against' as evidence that vaccines have made disease threats irrelevant.
distractors.why_wrong: That sentence is part of Allen's EXPLANATION of why people underestimate risk, not his recommendation. Step 2 shows the recommendation comes after, and says vaccine-preventable diseases ARE on the rise. Step 5 shows A inverts Allen's alarm.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at Allen's openness about 'risks and benefits' and read him as on the fence about vaccination overall.
distractors.why_wrong: Allen explicitly calls vaccines 'medicine's greatest lifesaver'; he wants ONE OF the parts of the public conversation to be risks. Step 3's paraphrase keeps him firmly pro-vaccine.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a passage about disease and vaccination is to project a 'new diseases will keep coming' theme.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't make any claim about the inevitability of new diseases. Step 5 shows D imports a topic the passage never raises.

host-2016-verb2-ELF-040.technique: On 'present situation' items, locate the AUTHOR'S RECOMMENDATION at the end of the passage and match the option that names that prescription. Words like 'will require', 'long overdue', 'need to' flag where the prescription sits.
host-2016-verb2-ELF-040.pitfall: Authors who frankly acknowledge risks of something they support get misread as ambivalent. Allen's 'risks AND benefits' framing is a call for honest discussion, not a doubt about overall value — read the surrounding sentences before assuming hedging.
